item.title: Re: Credit where credit is due
item.content:encoded: As there's no indication this image has been released under Creative Commons, the image has been removed.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edits
- Hide

Due to the existence of the site-88 hub, as well as other GRU-P content, the following two edits are being proposed.

Following Interview 2314-1, SCP-2314-1 was taken to Site-88 for further debriefing.

to

Following Interview 2314-1, SCP-2314-1 was taken to Site-88 for further debriefing.

and

SCP-2314-1 claims to have been a member of a GRU Division "P" Remote Viewing Division stationed at OKB-16.

to

SCP-2314-1 claims to have been a member of a GRU Division "P" Remote Viewing Division stationed at OKB-16.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for expert gif usage

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In some ways, I'm a fish in a barrel in terms of likely support for this article, so my preferences towards stories of Russian intrigue might be affecting my vote. As time goes on, I find myself less engaged by the interview in general as a narrative device. It's sort of a standard trick that writers include in many articles almost by default, and it's frequently a blunt instrument for characterization.

I like this deployment of the interview format, however, because of its necessity. Treated just on its own, the base anomaly would probably be one that I wouldn't be too terribly moved by, and I frequently advise people against stapling on more addenda and backstory to something in an attempt to make a concept interesting. In this case, the story is the main draw, and I would hazard a guess that it was conceived before the rest of the narrative, with the extra details in service to it.

What's interesting is that it doesn't even really give us much insight into the girl as a character, but what it really does is give us a) two nicely done, not-overworked moments of intimate dread on the parts of the Foundation staff, and b) a compelling window into the operations of the Foundation as an organization without being too explain-y. I wouldn't necessarily advise other writers to copy this, as such, but reading this does provide a pretty good example of when an interview is necessary to the article, and how something can provide reader engagement without overdoing it in the emotional department.

This one resonates, beyond the manner in which my somewhat predictable preferences lie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should be "Project Eyestalk".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Translation for the title please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing what Crayne said. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the praise. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot, I think the length was fine and so was the pacing. I knew what was going on half way through but the rest was emotional payoff that made me understand it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You make a very good point. Edited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the usage of the phrase 'turned up' at the start of the interview section is a bit informal. 'Arrived', or similar would be more inline with a foundation doc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woot woot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy +104, Nikolaus and me. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview was pretty long, like others have said, but not long enough to be a turn off for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This worked for me in the end, a lot of people have issues with the length of the interviews. I don't think the scip would have worked without the length based on the woman and her tribulations earlier in life regarding what happened, if it were shortened we wouldn't know anything. Gets my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shouldn't this have the parasite tag?

also, cool concept and decent writing that has a good hook

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the gif, I think your the only one who did that :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is substantially better than when I seen it last ,though I can't get but dislike certain features of the interview, which I don't feel the need to repeat since we have talked with Crayne about it extensively. (and I mean, even with your conclusion in place which is something we have in the end agreed to is a feasible way to do it when you explained. It's just that here it still comes off very much on the side of her being simply nuts rather than her sister having an actual presence)

That said, the state of matters is kinda my fault as I never in the end got to finish my variant of the interview, so eh.

No vote, partly because I like the description and concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with this - in fact for the longest time I didn't use contractions either by preference as I found them somewhat unnatural and hard to use appropriately.

Also, this finally got posted?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2314-1 not using contractions is actually a stylistic choice to emphasize the fact that she's very much ESL and has been hiding out on the run for both her own government and the Foundation.

As for the length of the interview, you're not the first to comment on that, so we may have to edit that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked most of it, but thought the interview was long and somewhat unrealistic. It could be edited. (Unrealistic: She doesn't use contractions- I found it hard to imagine someone speaking like that. Also, that there are way more ellipses then I would think of being put in an official transcript of an interview.) I would upvote if not for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the way you took a common cliché (Twins have telepathy with each other) and took it to its logical conclusion. Very creative, and very creepy from both the perspective of the two girls or the Foundation. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You lost me in the interview (tl;dr, to put it bluntly), but I do like the direction you took with the girl. Nice to see someone with a connection a skip like this (how does she 'receive' those images, anyway?) who isn't in it to demolish the Foundation or whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure if it was intentional or not, but I quite enjoyed the site-88 nod.

I enjoyed this outside of that as well: it tells a coherent interesting story and a good bit of world building is done through the narrative.

I know some folk like "more mystery" in their skips, but I don't think this would've benefited from that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral for now… The long text wall interview turned me off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this is an amazing skip. Although I do think that there was a bit too much exposition, the ending interview won my vote. Great job :)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted so much.

Alone any of the components could come off as cliche, but the combination, and the way that the full reveal unfolds, makes for a rather nice little roller coaster of twists. I love how the agent's reaction has so many extra levels of creepiness to it in light of what the Foundation learned about it afterward, and the reader already knows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm I dunno, this is written superbly and the idea is not bad at all…I just feel that too much was given, I don't feel much mystery with this, I'm not wanting more of it. I'm tempted to upvote for the writing and idea alone but I just feel this could've done with less exposition to make me wonder a bit more.

Neutral vote for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only nitpick with this one was that it felt a little too long for the amount of payoff we got out of it, but other than that it's a clever concept, a good use of the GOI and the quality of writing is excellent.

Well done, Nikolaus and Crayne.

item.title: Re: Credit where credit is due
item.content:encoded: I was going to upvote, but yeah, collab. Too bad, I want to upvote it. ;)

item.title: Credit where credit is due
item.content:encoded:
Major credit goes to Crayne for collaborating with me and helping me put my ideas on paper.
Also special thanks to users Djkaktus, VAElynx, Dr. Reach and Athena_Grey for critique in chat.

Wouldn't have posted without their help.

Picture credit goes to http://labs.bio.unc.edu/Goldstein/movies.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this thing so much. Excellent, excellent job. A fantastic first article.
